body:
Hey Marcel,

Following up on what we talked about Sunday — yeah, I've been using that NOAA buoy page you sent me, and it's a step up from what I had before. Checked it this morning around 5:15 before the coffee even finished and it had the wind reading about two knots off from what I actually saw at the dock. Close enough.

That said, I still want a backup for when the site loads slow. Out here in Bayport the signal does what it wants before sunrise. If you've got a recommendation for an app that caches the last few hours of data offline, I'm listening. Doesn't need to be fancy — wind speed, wave height, and the tide window is all I need before I decide whether Lucky Strike goes out.

Let me know when you get a minute.

Dad

Dad,

Glad the NOAA page is working better than the old setup. For offline caching, try Windy — it's free, pulls forecast data when you're on WiFi, and holds it for a few hours without a signal. The marine layer view on it is solid for Gulf Coast mornings. PredictWind is another option but it's overkill unless you're doing offshore runs.

For tide windows specifically, Tides Near Me works offline once it's loaded the local station data. Should pair well with whatever you're already doing at 5 AM. Download both tonight and poke around before next week's charters start.

Also, Ashley wanted me to remind you that Elise's birthday is the 12th. She's been asking if Captain Grandpa is bringing her fishing again this summer. Just passing that along.

Marcel

Marcel,

Had a guy call last night — Rick Holoway, says he booked a half-day for the 14th and never got a confirmation. Check the inbox and see if it bounced or went to his spam. He sounded reasonable, just wanted to know he was on the list before driving down to Bayport.

We push off from the harbor at 5 AM that morning. If he's confirmed, send him the pre-trip sheet and tell him to park at the Bayport ramp lot and look for Lucky Strike — white hull, blue stripe, hard to miss. Tides are running good that window, planning to run south toward Three Rocks by first light.

I'm unreachable once we're underway, so get it sorted before then. You still have the login for the booking platform?

— Dad

Dad,

Found him — booking went through fine on our end, but the confirmation email got caught in his spam filter. Resent it from the main address and attached the pre-trip sheet. He replied within the hour, confirmed for the 14th, no issues.

While I had the inbox open I noticed two other open inquiries sitting from last week. A family of four asking about a full-day on the 18th, and a T. Ruiz asking whether you take groups larger than six. I can draft replies on both if you want to review them tonight, or I can just handle the straightforward one.

Holding off on the Ruiz question until I hear from you — don't want to quote something wrong.

— Marcel

Marcel,

Good work on Holoway. Tell the family of four the 18th is open — full-day rate is $650, they bring their own lunch, fishing license is covered under the charter. Send them the standard terms.

Ruiz is a no. Lucky Strike is certified for six, that's the cap, Coast Guard doesn't care what he's willing to pay. Tell him six is the max and if his group is bigger I can't help him. Be polite about it but don't leave it open-ended.

I'll look at anything else Tuesday after dock cleanup. Tide window's tight that morning so we should be back at the harbor around one.

— Dad

Marcel,

Just tied up at the dock — it's about 2:20. Saw two missed calls from this morning and a voicemail I couldn't make out. Was running a group past the Three Sisters by 5:45, Yamaha 350s wide open, no signal past the buoy line until we turned back around noon. Decent morning — eight reds and a couple of flounder. Anyway, what'd you need? I've got maybe an hour before I start washing down the cockpit.

Dad

Dad,

Glad the trip went well. So here's the situation — a guy named Donnie Heard called the booking line around 8 this morning. He's on the July 12th half-day and wants to upgrade to a full-day and add two rods. Said he's been out with you before, redfish trip sometime last fall. I told him you'd call back after 2, but he said he's in meetings until 4. Also, somebody named Karen Pruitt left a voicemail asking about availability the last week of August — didn't sound like she'd booked before. I've got both numbers whenever you're ready. Let me know how you want to handle the Heard upgrade on the booking sheet.

Marcel

Marcel,

Donnie — yeah, I remember him. Caught a 28-inch red on a live pinfish and talked about it the whole ride back. Good guy. Full-day upgrade for four people puts him at $1,100 flat, same rate as always. Two extra rods are fine, the cockpit runs six no problem. Go ahead and update the sheet. I'll call him after 4 to confirm. Remind him to bring a cooler — last time he didn't and I had to double-bag his fish in a trash bag from the bait well. 

For Karen Pruitt, last week of August I've got the 26th and 28th open. Send her those dates and the half-day rate, $650 for up to four. If she wants more, she can call me direct any day after 2.

Dad

Dad,

All updated. Donnie already texted back — confirmed the full-day, he's bringing his brother-in-law, and he said to tell you he's not forgetting the cooler this time. I put the $1,100 on the sheet and marked it full-day for six. I forwarded Karen Pruitt the open dates and the rate about twenty minutes ago, no reply yet but I'll let you know when she responds. You're clear for tomorrow per the forecast — light chop, southeast wind at 8. Have a good one.

Marcel

Marcel,

Got your message from this morning — didn't see it until I was off the water. Had a full boat out to the Stumpknockers today, didn't dock Lucky Strike until just past two. By the time I finished the hose-down and fuel log it was nearly three-thirty.

Looked over the two invoices you forwarded from the Bennett Guitar Works account. The Stew-Mac order is fine, go ahead and approve it. The other one from the tonewoods supplier — hold off. I want to call them directly before anything goes out. Their shipping quote doesn't match what they quoted me in March.

Also, Diane mentioned you never confirmed dinner Sunday. She's making her shrimp and grits so don't make her cook for five if you're not coming.

Dad

Dad,

Got it. Stew-Mac approved, tonewoods supplier on hold. I flagged their invoice with a note so it won't process automatically. Let me know what they say when you call — if the rate changed I can pull the March email chain and put it in front of them.

One other thing: the Lucky Strike listing on the charter directory site still shows last year's pricing. I can update it remotely if you send me the new half-day and full-day rates whenever you get a chance. No rush, just don't want customers calling with the wrong number in their head.

And yes, I'll be there Sunday. Tell Diane to count me in. Ashley and the kids are coming too if that's alright — Elise has been asking about the boat all week so Beau probably guilted her into it.

Marcel

Marcel,

Following up on the Gmail API thing we talked about last week. I got the credentials set up like you walked me through, but when I try to pull emails into the booking sheet it's throwing an auth error — something about a scope being invalid. I copied the exact scope string you sent me.

I'm running this off the laptop in the workshop, not the boat rig, so it's not a connectivity issue. Error code says 403, access denied. Pasted the whole thing below.

Got charters running Tuesday through Friday this week, so mornings before 4:30 AM or after 2:30 PM is when I can actually sit down and work through this.

— Carlos

Dad,

Got it — that 403 is a scope authorization issue, not the credentials themselves. When you set up the OAuth consent screen in Google Cloud, the Gmail readonly scope needs to be explicitly added under the 'Scopes' section. If it wasn't added there, the token you generated won't include it even if the string looks right in the code.

Here's what to do: go back into your Google Cloud project, hit APIs & Services, then OAuth consent screen, then Edit App. Scroll to 'Add or Remove Scopes' and search for gmail.readonly — check that box and save. After that you'll need to delete the token.json file (if one was created) and re-run the auth flow so it generates a fresh token with the right scope baked in.

Let me know what you see after that. Should take about ten minutes. If you get a new error code I can dig further.

— Marcel

Marcel,

That did it. Deleted the old token, ran through the auth flow again, and it pulled in the last 30 days of booking confirmation emails without a problem. About time — I've been logging those by hand into the spreadsheet since April.

I'll wire up the rest of it this week during the off-charter hours. Diane thinks I'm going overboard automating a fishing charter, but she also thought the GPS plotter was overkill in 2009 and I haven't run aground since.

Appreciate you sticking with it. Tell Ashley and the kids hello.

— Dad

Marcel,

Following up on that Google Calendar API issue you were helping me sort out last week. Still getting the 403 error when the booking form tries to write events to the Lucky Strike calendar. Tested it again this morning before I headed out — same result. The OAuth credentials are in there, scope is set to calendar.events, but it keeps kicking back "insufficient permissions" on the POST.

I need this working before the weekend. Got four charters booked Saturday and Sunday and right now I'm still writing everything down on paper like it's 1987. If you've got a fix, send it plain — I'm on the water most of the day and reading on a phone screen in full sun isn't exactly ideal.

Carlos

Dad,

Got you. The 403 is almost certainly because the service account was authorized with read-only scope at some point and the token got cached. The fix is straightforward: go into Google Cloud Console, pull up the OAuth consent screen for the Lucky Strike project, and make sure the scope listed is https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar — not calendar.readonly. After that, revoke and reissue the credentials, re-download the JSON key file, and drop it back into whatever path your .env is pointing at.

Also check that the calendar ID you're targeting is the actual calendar ID (long string ending in @group.calendar.google.com), not just the display name. That trips people up more than anything. Let me know what you find when you're back at the dock.

Marcel

Marcel,

That was it — calendar.readonly was the scope. Changed it, pulled new credentials, dropped the JSON in. Booking form wrote a test event clean on the first try. Elise would say I should celebrate. I'm going to call it even and move on.

One more thing while I have you: the events are showing up in UTC. Charters leave Bayport at 5 AM local and I don't want clients reading the calendar thinking departure is 10 AM. Is there a timeZone field I pass in the event body, or does that get set somewhere at the calendar level?

Carlos

Dad,

Glad it's writing. For the timezone, you set it at the event level in the body — both the start and end objects take a timeZone field. Pass "America/Chicago" in each one alongside your dateTime string and Google will display it correctly in whatever timezone the viewer is in while storing it properly on the backend. Looks like this: { "dateTime": "2026-03-31T05:00:00", "timeZone": "America/Chicago" }. Do that for both start and end.

You can also set a default timezone on the calendar itself under Settings in Google Calendar, but doing it per-event is more reliable if you ever need to handle anything outside the Gulf Coast. Test it with tomorrow's trip and let me know if it lands right. Tell Diane I said hey.

Marcel

Hi Marcel,

Thanks for the note. Tomorrow afternoon works — does 3pm fit?

Best,
Carlos

3pm is great. Sending the calendar invite now.

Marcel

Marcel,

Following up on the OpenWeather API issue I mentioned last week. Still getting stale wind data when I pull forecasts through the app — it's showing conditions from two, sometimes three hours back. During charter season that's not just annoying, it's a liability. I've got clients on the boat by 5 AM and I need current readings off Bayport before we clear the harbor.

The API key is active and the calls are going through — I can see them in the dashboard. But the data timestamp isn't matching real time. Checked it this morning against the NOAA buoy and I was 12 knots off.

What's the fix here?

Carlos

Hey Dad,

Sorry this is dragging on. The timestamp issue is almost certainly a caching problem on your end, not the API itself. OpenWeather's free tier caches responses for up to 10 minutes, but if you've got any local caching layer or a service worker sitting in front of your requests, you can stack that lag fast. Two to three hours sounds like something is aggressively caching and not invalidating.

Can you tell me how you're making the API call? Is this a browser-based app, something running on your phone, or a script on a laptop? Also, are you hitting the /current endpoint or /onecall? That'll help me narrow it down.

If you're on the free plan we can also look at upgrading to One Call 3.0 — it's a few bucks a month but the data latency is much tighter and it'd be worth it for what you're using it for.

Let me know and I'll get you sorted.

Marcel

Marcel,

It's running on the old Dell laptop I keep in the wheelhouse — Python script, hits the /current endpoint. No browser involved. I don't think I set up any caching intentionally but I honestly couldn't tell you for sure; Luc helped me put it together two seasons ago.

I'll take the upgrade if it tightens the latency. A few dollars a month is nothing compared to turning back early because I read the conditions wrong. Send me what I need to switch it over and I'll handle it tonight after I'm off the water.

Carlos

Marcel,

Got your voicemail from yesterday. Yes, the routine is the same as it's been for years — up around 4:30, coffee first, then I'm on Windy and the NOAA buoy feed for the Chassahowitzka before the sun's even thinking about it. I cross that with the VHF marine forecast on 162.400 and check the Crystal River buoy for actual wave height, not the modeled stuff. Takes about twenty minutes. If all three line up, Lucky Strike leaves the Bayport dock on schedule. If they don't, I'm calling clients before 5:15.

You asked whether I ever just trust one source. No. That's how you put clients in 4-foot chop on a day the app called glassy.

Dad

Dad,

That makes sense, and it's actually why I was asking. I've been looking at a marine weather aggregator called PredictWind that pulls NOAA, Windy, and buoy data into one screen with threshold alerts — you set your wind speed and wave height limits and it flags the morning window automatically. Some charter operators up in Tampa Bay use it.

There's a free tier, and the offshore layer with buoy overlays runs $12.99/month. Thought it might cut the twenty-minute check down, especially on the early mornings where you're toggling between three tabs half-asleep. Worth a look?

Marcel

Marcel,

Thirteen dollars a month to do what I'm already doing in twenty minutes and a cup of coffee — I'll keep my money. The toggling isn't the problem. The cross-checking is the whole point. One source gives you a forecast. Three sources give you a decision.

If you want to find me something useful, look into a reliable current predictor for the Homosassa flats that uses the USGS river discharge data, not just the tide tables. I called a tide window wrong by forty minutes last Thursday and missed a redfish flat that would've put clients on fish all morning. That one actually costs me.

Dad

Fair point on the coffee math. Won't bring up PredictWind again.

On the Homosassa currents — I did some looking. Tides Near Me Pro has a layer that integrates USGS discharge gauges from the Homosassa and Crystal rivers, so it accounts for the freshwater push that throws the standard tables off. A few Nature Coast guides mentioned it in a forum thread. Free version is basic tides only; full version is $29 a year. That math I think you'll accept.

I'll text you the link. Ashley and the kids are coming down to Bayport the weekend of the 12th — Beau's been asking about fishing off the dock again. Tell Diane hey.

Marcel

Marcel,

Got your message. Quick update on this morning — we pushed off right at 5:15, had a full boat of six out of Bayport. Tide window was tight but we worked the grass flats around the mouth of the Weeki Wachee and put fish in the box. Decent red drum day, couple of nice flounder. Back at the dock by 1:45, which is about right for a Tuesday in June.

Reason I'm following up is the port engine on Lucky Strike has been running a little rough at idle since last Thursday. Nothing that stopped us today, but I'm not going to ignore it heading into the weekend — I've got charters booked Friday through Sunday. Going to pull the plugs tomorrow morning before I head out and see if it's a fouling issue. If it's the injectors again I'll have Paulie at the marina take a look.

Let me know if you needed anything else from me on this. Tell Ashley and the kids hey.

Dad

Dad,

Glad the day went well — six clients and fish in the box is a good Tuesday no matter how you cut it. Red drum on the flats is hard to beat.

On the engine, that sounds like it's worth staying ahead of. If Paulie's backed up and you need a second opinion, Mike Torrens at Gulf Coast Marine in Spring Hill has done right by a few guys I know up here. He's good on the Yamaha four-strokes especially. Either way, don't let it slide into the weekend with a full charter load — not worth the headache out on the water.

Ashley says hi back. Elise has been asking when she can come out on Lucky Strike again, so maybe once things slow down in November we can work something out. Beau just wants to hold a rod, which honestly sounds about right for a four-year-old.

Let me know what Paulie finds.

Marcel

Marcel,

Sorry I missed your call this morning. Once Lucky Strike clears the channel marker out of Bayport, there's no signal until I'm back at the dock — that's the whole window from about five to two, nothing I can do about it. This time of year I've got a full cockpit most days, so it's not changing anytime soon.

What did you need? I've got some time right now before I start wiping down the cockpit and checking the bilge. Send me another email or try me on the phone after two if it comes up again.

— Dad

Dad,

No worries, I figured you were out on the water. I was calling because a guy named Thornton reached out wanting to book a half-day — him and three other guys, specifically asking about redfish on the grass flats. He said he filled out the booking form on the site twice and never got a confirmation email back either time. He's flexible but wants to go out in the next two weeks.

I tested the form myself and I think the confirmation mailer is broken — nothing came to my inbox when I submitted a test. Can you check with whoever manages the site? Once we know it's fixed I can call Thornton back with available dates.

— Marcel

Marcel,

That mailer's been on my list since April. Call Thornton back today if you can — tell him we've got Thursday and Saturday mornings open, and get a deposit to hold the date. Half-day runs $425, Venmo or check, same as always.

I'll get the web guy on the phone this week about the form. And next time something's broken on the site, don't wait on me — just shoot me a text the night before so I see it before I'm out on the water.

— Dad

Marcel,

Got back around 1:45 today — decent run, three reds and more trout than I expected for a Tuesday. Got the Lucky Strike cleaned up and sat down to go through messages.

Still waiting on word from that Yamaha dealer about the starboard F150. You said Tuesday you were going to follow up with them about the water pump impeller. Did anything come of that? I've got a group of six booked Saturday for a full-day run and I need to know if I'm looking at a delay before I make promises.

Not pressing, just need a heads-up when you know something.

— Dad

Dad,

Apologies — it's been a week over here. I called Sea Ray Marine on Cortez Road this morning. They've got the impeller in stock: Yamaha part 6H3-44352-02-00, about $28 plus roughly an hour labor. Spoke to a guy named Derek. He said he can fit you in Thursday afternoon or Friday morning.

I'd call Derek direct at (941) 555-0192 and grab Friday morning before someone else does. That clears you for Saturday with room to spare.

Let me know if you need me to handle anything else on this end.

— Marcel

Marcel,

Friday morning works. Called Derek — 8 AM, he'll have it done by noon. Saturday should be fine.

While I had him on the phone I asked about the raw water strainer too. Basket's been showing some scale buildup I don't love. He said same visit, maybe another $15 in parts, no problem. Told him go ahead.

Appreciate you running that down. Tell Ashley and the kids hello. Elise still after you about that fishing trip?

— Dad

Dad,

Glad it worked out. I put Friday 8 AM in my calendar — I'll check in with you after to make sure everything went smooth.

Elise has not let it go for one day. She drew a picture of herself standing on the Lucky Strike holding a fish that's bigger than the boat. I'll send you a photo. Beau caught wind of it and now he wants to come too, so you may be running a two-kid charter whether you planned on it or not.

We're mostly free in July. Ashley has the 12th and 13th off if you want to pick a date.

— Marcel
